content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Recently, I spent a lot of time working with Meta APIs and going through the app review process.
At first, I thought the difficult part would be building the application.
I was wrong.
Building the app was easy.
Getting permissions approved was the real challenge.
After several failures, rejections, and countless hours of trial and error, I learned some lessons that I wish somebody had told me earlier.
Hopefully, these lessons save someone else from making the same mistakes.
Build a Meaningful Application
Meta reviewers do not want to see a random demo app.
Build something that actually solves a problem.
Your application does not need to be huge, but it should make sense.
Show how users will use it and why the permissions you request are necessary.
Create an MVP with Every Feature Working
Your app does not need to be perfect, but every feature related to the requested permissions should work properly. Broken buttons, unfinished screens, or missing functionality make it difficult for reviewers to understand your use case.
You do not need to implement everything like payments or subscriptions. For example, if your app is an AI image generator, simply showing image creation, fetching, updating, and deletion is enough.
Most importantly, always start your screencast from a fresh login and a fresh OAuth flow. Never skip directly to an already connected account. Show the complete journey from authorization to the actual feature that uses the permissions.
Keep it simple, but make it functional.
Avoid OAuth Sign-Up During App Review
This mistake cost me time.
Do not make it difficult for reviewers to access your app. Avoid requiring Google OAuth, two-factor authentication, or other unnecessary steps during app review. The simpler the login process, the better.
Make it easy for reviewers to access the application without unnecessary friction.
Keep Screencast Videos Short
Nobody wants to watch a twenty-minute video.
Try to keep your screencast around five minutes.
Focus only on what matters.
Reviewers want to understand the flow quickly.
Record Your Voice
Explain every step while recording.
Do not assume reviewers understand what you are doing.
Tell them exactly what button you clicked and why.
A voice explanation makes the review process much easier.
Add Captions
Captions are probably one of the most important parts of the review process. In my experience, unclear videos often lead to rejection because reviewers can easily miss important steps.
Do not rely only on audio. Add captions with tools like CapCut and explain every action on the screen. Write simple captions like:
“Now we are logging in.”
“This is our dashboard.”
“We are clicking the Connect Meta Account button.”
“Now we are granting the required permissions.”
These captions make it much easier for reviewers to follow the flow and understand exactly why your app needs each permission.
Always Show the OAuth Flow
Do not skip the OAuth flow. Before recording each permission screencast, first remove your app from the Facebook account so you start with a completely fresh authorization.
Then begin the recording from the start. Show the user connecting their Facebook account, display the consent screen, and continue all the way to the actual feature that uses the permission.
Do not simply click a “Reconnect” button on an already authorized account. Make sure you are performing a completely fresh OAuth flow every time.
Reviewers want to see the complete journey, not an account that is already connected.
Pause on Permission Screens
This is something I learned after rejection.
When the permission screen appears, pause for five to ten seconds.
Slowly scroll through all requested permissions.
Make sure reviewers can clearly see every permission your app asks for.
Never Skip the Code Exchange Step
Do not magically appear inside the application after login.
Show the complete OAuth process.
Authorization.
Redirect.
Code exchange.
Access token generation.
Then continue to the app.
Skipping these steps may create confusion.
Demonstrate the Exact Use Case
The video should justify every permission you request.
If a permission is only used for reading data, then show data being fetched and displayed properly in the UI.
Nothing more.
If the permission allows Create, Read, Update, and Delete operations, then demonstrate all four actions.
Create something.
View it.
Modify it.
Delete it.
Make the relationship between the permission and the feature obvious.
My Biggest Lesson
I used to think Meta app approval was mostly about writing code.
But I discovered that communication matters just as much.
The reviewer cannot read your mind.
They only see your application and your screencast.
So make their job easy.
Explain everything.
Show every step.
Pause when necessary.
And remember that clarity is often more important than complexity.
Hopefully, these lessons save someone else the days of frustration that I went through.
Because sometimes the most valuable knowledge comes from failing first.
Be Patient and Don’t Lose Hope
With the rise of AI-generated and vibe-coded apps, Meta seems to be reviewing more carefully, and response times have become longer. What used to take around 10 days can now take 20 days or more.
Most importantly, do not lose hope if your app gets rejected.
My first app was rejected twice and only got approved on the third attempt. In fact, those rejections taught me everything I shared in this article.
So be patient, carefully read the feedback, improve your app and screencasts, and try again. Sometimes approval is not about getting everything right on the first try — it’s about learning from each rejection and coming back stronger.
Thanks.
